steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Need to filter /var/log/app/access.log down to only requests that hit our internal API endpoints — anything matching `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/` — and dump those lines to /home/user/internal_hits.log. But here's the thing: we're also seeing a ton of health check spam from the load balancer that I want excluded, those come from 10.0.0.0/8 range and hit `/health` or `/ready`. So internal API calls yes, but not if they're from the 10.x.x.x health probes.

Also the timestamps in that log are in that annoying Apache combined format with the brackets, would be nice if the output had them as ISO 8601 instead (like 2024-01-15T14:30:22). Rest of the line can stay as-is.

Log's about 50k lines so nothing crazy but I don't want to sit here doing it by hand.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
user@1cfb3d26d3c7:~$

Analysis: I'm starting fresh in the home directory. I need to: 1) Check if the log file exists at /var/log/app/access.log, 2) Filter for lines matching `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/`, 3) Exclude requests from 10.0.0.0/8 IP range, 4) Convert Apache combined format timestamps to ISO 8601, and 5) Write the output to /home/user/internal_hits.log.
Plan: First, I'll check if the log file exists and examine a few sample lines to understand its format. Then I'll craft a solution using grep and awk/sed to filter and transform the data. The approach will be: 1) Use grep to filter for internal API endpoints, 2) Use grep -v to exclude 10.x.x.x addresses, 3) Use awk or sed to convert the timestamp format from [DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +ZONE] to ISO 8601 format.

results.content:
Previous response had warnings:

34.28.48.212 - - [05/May/2024:10:23:56 +0000] "PATCH /api/v1/public/data HTTP/1.1" 400 5873 "-" "Python-urllib/3.9"
10.86.51.19 - - [27/Aug/2024:10:40:43 +0000] "GET /ready HTTP/1.1" 204 2407 "-" "ELB-HealthChecker/2.0"

steps.message:
Analysis: Good! The log file exists and is about 5.9MB (around 50k lines as mentioned). I can see the Apache combined log format with timestamps like [05/Dec/2024:10:07:16 +0000]. I can see examples of health checks from 10.x.x.x IPs hitting /ready. Now I need to see some examples of internal API endpoints to confirm the pattern.
Plan: Let me search for lines that match the internal API pattern to see what they look like. Then I'll build a pipeline that: 1) Filters for lines matching `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/`, 2) Excludes lines starting with 10.x.x.x IPs, 3) Converts timestamps from [DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +ZONE] to ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS), 4) Writes to /home/user/internal_hits.log

grep: write error: Broken pipe

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! I can see internal API endpoints with various version numbers (v1, v2, v3). I notice the 5th line has an IP starting with 10.239.x.x which should be excluded per the requirements. Now I'll build the complete filtering and transformation pipeline.
Plan: I'll create a pipeline that: 1) Uses grep to find lines matching `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/`, 2) Uses grep -v to exclude lines starting with '10.' (the 10.0.0.0/8 range), 3) Uses awk to convert the timestamp format. The timestamp conversion needs to map month names to numbers (Jan->01, Feb->02, etc.) and reformat from [DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +ZONE] to YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS. I'll write the result to /home/user/internal_hits.log.

Analysis: The command executed successfully! The pipeline has filtered the log file for internal API endpoints, excluded 10.x.x.x IPs, converted timestamps to ISO 8601 format, and written the results to /home/user/internal_hits.log. Now I should verify the output to ensure it worked correctly.
Plan: I'll check the output file to verify: 1) The file was created and has content, 2) The timestamps are in ISO 8601 format, 3) Only internal API endpoints are present, 4) No 10.x.x.x IPs are included. I'll also count the lines to see how many matched our criteria.

steps.message:
Analysis: Excellent! The filtering and transformation worked perfectly. The output file has 4,800 lines (filtered down from ~50k). I can see: 1) Timestamps are now in ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS), 2) All lines contain internal API endpoints (/api/v1/internal/, /api/v2/internal/, /api/v3/internal/), 3) No IPs starting with '10.' are present (I see 192.168.x.x, 52.x.x.x, 185.x.x.x, 198.x.x.x), 4) The rest of the log line format is preserved. Let me do a final verification to ensure no 10.x.x.x IPs slipped through.
Plan: I'll do a final check to confirm no 10.x.x.x IPs are in the output file, and also verify that we're capturing different API versions (v1, v2, v3) correctly. Then the task will be complete.

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! The task has been completed successfully. The verification confirms: 1) Zero lines with IPs starting with '10.' (health check traffic from 10.0.0.0/8 range successfully excluded), 2) All three API versions are captured (v1: 1,833 lines, v2: 1,753 lines, v3: 1,214 lines = 4,800 total), 3) Timestamps are properly converted to ISO 8601 format throughout the file, 4) All entries match the internal API endpoint pattern. The filtered log has been successfully written to /home/user/internal_hits.log.
Plan: Task is complete. The file /home/user/internal_hits.log now contains only internal API requests (matching /api/v[0-9]+/internal/) with health check traffic from the 10.0.0.0/8 range excluded, and timestamps converted from Apache combined format to ISO 8601 format.

steps.message:
Analysis: Yes, I am confident the task is complete. The solution successfully: 1) Filtered /var/log/app/access.log for lines matching `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/` (internal API endpoints), 2) Excluded all requests from 10.0.0.0/8 IP range (load balancer health checks), 3) Converted timestamps from Apache combined format [DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +ZONE] to ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS), 4) Preserved the rest of each log line as-is, 5) Output 4,800 filtered lines to /home/user/internal_hits.log. All verification checks passed with zero 10.x.x.x IPs in the output and proper capture of v1, v2, and v3 API endpoints.
Plan: No further action needed. The task requirements have been fully satisfied.
